The weirdest thing about all of this to me is how much people underestimate what our brains are actually doing. They hear "The model is taking all the music and then remixing it" and ask "how is that different from what we do?" and I don't even know how to respond to that. We experience the world through emotions. When something inspires us to create it isn't often as simple as "I like that chord progression, I will use it" it's something much more complicated. Songs connect with us, our memories, our experiences, and it inspires us to express that feeling. All of that is so far beyond our own understanding LET ALONE something we can artificially replicate.
It is interesting to see technology bump up against philosophy though. I've never had to think so much about what a work of art being "Soulless" really means.

I just got back into music in July (2025) after a long period of other (family) commitments. I spent 15 years of my life taking care of my mom 24/7. She had dementia and died in March. 

What I've been doing is taking a song or song idea that I have written on guitar and composing it on Guitar Pro software. Some old songs, some new. Sometimes I will dream a song and as soon as I wake up, immediately go to my guitar to try to recreate the song or melody that is in my head. No matter how trivial the song or lyrics may seem at the time. Sometimes the melody is fleeting and if I don't work out the song right away, it will just escape me and I can't ever seem to get the right rhythm and melody back. 

Once I get the song down, the first thing I do is work out the tempo, then start composing on Guitar Pro, which uses MIDI and RSE (realistic sound engine) to create the sounds of the instruments. I always use the RSE sounds. I compose multi-tracks, usually rhythm and lead guitar, bass, drums, sometimes synthesizer and a vocal melody track. I've been using a jazz piano RSE for the vocal melody. Once I'm happy with the composition, I'll export an mp3 of the composition, then upload the mp3 to SUNO for AI to interpret my song. 

The vocal melody track has been the most problematic thing to try to get AI to understand that THIS is the melody that goes with the lyrics that I have written. I've had some pretty decent results, though. 

So far I have only ever done one song where I only wrote the lyrics and had AI completely compose the music. It is in a post-hardcore style. 

I went through a creative period in July and August and in the period of about a month, from the end of July to the end of August, I published 10 of my own songs (which I label in the caption "original music and lyrics, AI-interpreted"), plus that one AI-composed song where I only wrote the lyrics. I published 3 versions of one of my songs, one alt version and one extended version with lyrics from a 19th century poem (that is in the public domain), which I used to perform at open mic. So 13 published songs altogether.  

I haven't done a whole lot musically in September or October. I've written a few songs, one as recently as yesterday (16 oct 2025), but I haven't published any of them yet. 

It's a cheap way of at least putting out a version of my songs without having to record them in the traditional manner.

I really like Suno, but in some situations I end up spending a lot of credits trying to get close to the result I actually need. For example, in some versions of songs I generate in Suno, I really like the vocal interpretation, but when I separate the voice from the instrumental it naturally comes with artifacts. I did a lot of research and found that there are some ways to improve this, such as remastering in the most current model or creating a cover, but the best results I’ve had so far were precisely by creating covers. Many times, in the cover, I ask Suno to recreate the a cappella, meaning to sing it again while imitating the tone, but without adding any extra effects or elements, as if it were a clean studio recording, in raw mode. The problem is that it’s very difficult to achieve this consistently, and usually I need many attempts before I can use small sections of it.

The same difficulty happens with some instrumental elements. For example, sometimes I like a pad in a certain version, but when I separate it, it comes mixed with other instruments or melodies. Then I try to recreate it in cover mode in an isolated way, but the result is not as faithful to the progression, timing, or tone. This also happens when I record a guitar solo and ask Suno to make its version of my solo in an isolated way. In some cases, it even delivers a wonderful tone, but then it starts mixing in other things that shouldn’t be there, not respecting the isolated request. The same happens with backing vocals: even when I separate the stems, to get a clearer and higher-quality result I need to use the cover option, but again I face difficulties in keeping the purity of the execution.

Another issue is the difference between model versions. I managed to get better results with isolation prompts in version 4, but the audio quality in that version is not comparable to versions 4.5, 4.5 Plus, or version 5. This creates a limitation, because with the older model I can isolate better, but I don’t get the same audio quality that the newer versions provide.

As a music producer, I create my arrangements, I play the instruments, but I don’t sing. So it would be extremely useful to be able to use voices generated in Suno in raw mode, keeping only the interpretation without any extra processing, so that I could insert them directly into professional productions. The same would apply to other isolated elements, as if they were clean samples of specific instruments. Right now, I end up spending a lot of credits trying to achieve this, and most of the time I can only get small usable sections.

That’s why I would like to know if I am doing something wrong in the current workflow, if there is already a way to achieve this level of isolation in Suno, or if this is still a feature that needs to be improved in the platform so that it becomes possible.

I'm a Premium subscriber. I want to thank you for this incredible software. It's an unbelievably helpful, valuable, and exciting tool. There are a few things I am hoping for in the near future. The first is the ability to keep the track, and/or vocals the same as you iterate. When I almost have exactly what I want, I have found it impossible to get the same take, but with updated lyrics, even using the Audio 100% setting, and prompting for the exact same tonality, phrasing, vibrato, pitch, and expression of the vocalist. The second request is to be able to provide a karaoke version, rather than only an altered cover. Essentially, I'd love a note-for-note reproduction of the track, but with Suno v5's updated sounds, mix, and mastering. I have many old beats, and riffs which will sound much better, and much more natural with your software's sounds. I just don't want it to unexpectedly 'creatively' change scales/modes on me. The last request has to do with timing the vocals. It's most evident in rap tracks. Suno can't know where I want the syllables to land, so would it be possible to provide a bar/timing grid so we can dictate where the words land, and achieve the rhythm we desire? I know this can be done currently by recording a performance of the rap, then converting it, but it would be helpful not to need to perform that step. Once again, I'm so appreciative for what it offers already. Please see this simply as feedback, not as criticism, or a complaint.

First, thanks for doubling back with the more specific explanation. So this functions primarily as a representation adjustment (allowing a 3/4 piece to be displayed correctly instead of forced into 4/4). That’s helpful for notation and more easily editing/production, but it ignores the compositional aspects. A suggestion/request/plea? Meter is so foundational and can so extend the expressive range (composers, performers, etc...) it would be a total game changer if time signature could become a compositional parameter rather than just a display parameter. Meter is foundational, not just visually, but structurally. So, what would be extraordinary is if users could: Define meter as part of the structural DNA of the piece; Insert alternating bars (e.g., 3/4, 3/4, 3/4, 11/8, 7/8); Build sections in different meters (Verse in 7/8, Chorus in 4/4); Layer parts in contrasting meters (polymeter) This would move Suno beyond a 4/4 pop sensibility and into a much broader compositional landscape. There are iconic examples that show how powerful this is: Mission: Impossible Theme (Lalo Schifrin) – 5/4 The Rite of Spring (Stravinsky) Take Five (Brubeck) – 5/4 Money (pink floyd) – 7/4 verses Black Dog (Led Zeppelin) – shifting meter Here Comes the Sun (Beatles) – alternating bars of 11/8, 4/4, 7/8 A million different jazz/fusion tunes A ton of Indian classical music and konnakol tradition explores rhythmic cycles far beyond 4/4. Even allowing structured compound meters (6/8 vs 3/4 distinction) would dramatically expand expressive range.I recognize that most folks operate in 4/4 and some 3/4, and that’s commercially logical. But enabling meter as a creative control would open Suno to composers, jazz musicians, film scorers, and rhythmically adventurous artists in a way no AI systems currently do. For those of us who think rhythmically, it would be transformative.Thanks for considering!

CLARIFICATIONS #1 - Suno at no point holds Copyright on Anything. The portions You produce (lyrics you write, directions for tone/etc) are Your copyrighted material. And the AI-Generated portions are not allowed any legal form of copyright. If you give your lyrics and allow Suno to produce the music and sing it, You still own the copyright to your Song, but you do not have a copyright on this particular performance of it. (and if your song was used to make it illegally, the person who did can be in big trouble) If you direct Suno in significant form, beyond just the lyrics - staging the voice, intonation, inflections for specific lyrics, Key and shifts, tempo, ... the whole thing. You can argue in court that the work bears Significant Human Work, and in some cases obtain copyright over it. --- That said #2 - You entered a Contract with Suno when you made your account that set the limits on how you would use the resulting materials. Use of them in ways prohibited in that contract open you to lawsuit by Suno, not for copyright violation, but for contractual violation that can include the perceived dollar value of the produced song(s)... and/or their contribution of value to whatever they're used in/for. So they could sue against ad revenue for videos using the songs against the contract's rules, as example. ... That said, Even Free users are allowed to post videos for non-commercial use, such as a simple YT video of say, themselves dancing. And nothing prohibits someone Else from taking that video, and the music on it, to commercial purpose.

Your music will be flagged for the public to see if it’s ai generated. 2: Suno can’t guarantee copyright because ai music can’t be copyrighted by law unless it has sufficient human involvement. Which basically means most of the music has to be created by a human.

Which is why dialing in the output by maximizing input is so important in talking Upload your own Melody's, use Meta tags, dial in your style prompts on to the exact sub genre and like aesthetic BPM and everything, and write your own lyrics, the more detail you give it about your vision, the more accurate to what you wanted to make in, the less likely it is to create something that is kind of like excuse from a song it was trained on etc. Glazing input guarantees maximizesn quality of output that's just common sense

Another tip I have found is if you find a track you really like (especially true for covers of prior work), you can use the editor to replace the verse or chorus until you get a better pronunciation. This can also be used just to change up the phrasing on a verse or chorus for more interest. This leads to sonic fidelity issues, like dropping from wide to narrow fields but that's ok, because save your corrected version out and then run a cover of it and this will smooth over the seams so it sounds like a more cohesive song. Yes, the cover won't be exactly like the original, but you can make it closer by turning up the adherence to source higher, but it definitely removes the seams created from using the editor.

This only works because distributors like say distrokid or tube core and dsps like YouTube and Spotify have blanket licenses for the music uploaded on their platform only. You still need to purchase a cover license and any other applicable licenses such as mechanicals for each copy (every single stream, download or sale is counted as 1 mechanical or copy) for any use outside of that platform. Cover licensing is provided as a part of those services but can still be purchased independently and is the easiest followed by the mechanicals for an independent artist to obtain by simply buy it from the appropriate licensing entity. AI creations can’t be copyrighted without a substantial amount of human contribution to be determined by the copyright board that exists under the intellectual property board in your country. ContentID is not copyright and does not provide any copyright protection it only serves as identification and protection of content on digital platforms that use it either on their platform strictly or across the web. Copyright is literally a right that humans have to protect the thoughts in our head once they have been moved from your head into the real world in some way that makes them tangible or accessible to others and a song operates similar to how physical property of say a house. You pay an upfront fee and make continuous payments to keep using that property or asset.
